summary:
The most significant development in US data center policy this week was Governor Janet Mills' veto of Maine's LD 307 on April 24, 2026 — blocking what would have been the first statewide data center moratorium in US history. The bill had passed the legislature (House 79-62, Senate 21-13) and would have banned facilities over 20 MW until November 2027. Mills indicated she would have signed if the bill included a carve-out for the planned Jay data center at the former Androscoggin Mill site. In a notable parallel move, she simultaneously signed LD 713, which excludes data centers from Maine's business development tax incentive programs, and announced plans to issue an executive order creating a council to study data center impacts. The veto suggests governors are increasingly willing to draw a line between targeted regulations and broad moratoriums.

Beyond Maine, the broader state-level pattern remains gridlocked. New York S9144 (3-year moratorium on 20 MW+ facilities) and Vermont S.205 (moratorium through 2030) remain in committee. Michigan HB 4521 faces opposition from Governor Whitmer. Pennsylvania HB 2150 (environmental reporting requirements) passed the House 133-68 on April 13 and is now in the Senate. Arizona HB 2756 passed the House and cleared a Senate committee. At the local level, Denver's City Council adopted a one-year moratorium on new data center construction effective May 21, 2026, citing Stage 1 drought conditions. Oklahoma City approved a moratorium through end of 2026 on April 21. North Carolina continues to accumulate local actions, with Rowan County, Orange County, and the Town of Apex all enacting one-year moratoriums in April 2026. The Ohio Ballot Board approved a signature-collection initiative for a constitutional amendment that would ban data centers over 25 MW.

On the construction side, Vantage broke ground in April 2026 on its 1,200-acre Shackelford County, Texas campus for OpenAI's Stargate program — a 1.4 GW facility projected to cost $25 billion. Meta held a groundbreaking on April 21 for its $1B+ Project Anthem campus in East Tulsa, Oklahoma (its 28th US facility), despite Tulsa's active moratorium. Applied Digital signed a $7.5 billion, 15-year lease with an unnamed US hyperscaler for 300 MW at its Delta Forge 1 campus near Alexandria, Louisiana on April 23. Meta's Hyperion campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana remains under active construction with approximately 3,700 workers, backed by a $27 billion joint venture. The OpenAI Stargate Abilene campus is on track for full completion mid-2026, while the adjacent Crusoe/Microsoft 900 MW AI factory campus targets mid-2027.

The political dynamics are clarifying: no state has yet enacted a broad statewide moratorium, but targeted legislation is gaining traction in multiple chambers. The Maine veto illustrates the tension between moratorium advocates and governors focused on economic development — a pattern likely to repeat as similar bills advance in New York, Vermont, and Wisconsin. Meanwhile, the construction boom is accelerating faster than regulatory frameworks can keep pace, with a new generation of gigawatt-scale campuses breaking ground across Texas, Louisiana, and Oklahoma.
